text: Hello, I am Dr. Sanjay Mandal, GI surgeon from Kolkata and today I will be showing a video of laparoscopic cholecystectomy in a patient of situs inversus. This patient was a sixty six year old lady who had presented with pain in the left upper quadrant of the abdomen and on investigation it was revealed that she had situs inversus with a thick walled gallbladder with multiple stones.

The surgical setup is shown with the patient positioned legs apart, the surgeon standing between the legs, the camera holder on the right side, and the assistant on the left side. The monitor is positioned towards the patient's left shoulder, and a diagram illustrates the modified port positions for the procedure.

Upon entering the abdomen, the altered anatomy is visible with the liver positioned on the left side, the fundus of the stomach on the right, and the pylorus on the left. The first working port is a 5mm epigastric port used to locate the gallbladder, while additional working ports are placed on the left side below the left costal margin in the mid-clavicular and anterior axillary lines.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis to carefully remove significant pericholecystic adhesions, exposing the gallbladder amidst inflamed tissue. The altered anatomy due to situs inversus adds complexity to the procedure.

The surgical setup includes multiple five millimeter working ports positioned on the left side of the abdomen, providing flexibility for conversion to ten millimeter ports for clipping the cystic duct and artery during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

The surgeon uses the mid clavicular port as the main working port, which will be converted to a ten millimeter port for clipping the cystic artery and duct. Blunt and sharp dissection techniques are employed to separate the duodenum from the Hartmann's pouch of the gallbladder, exposing the Calot's triangle. Posterior dissection is performed to enhance the exposure of Calot's triangle.

The surgeon uses hook diathermy to take down flimsy adhesions, facilitating the dissection process. The duodenum is finally separated from the gallbladder, exposing the Calot's triangle and its structures.

The surgeon performs a meticulous sharp and blunt dissection of the Calot's triangle to expose the cystic duct and cystic artery. The suction cannula is used effectively for blunt dissection in the presence of acute inflammation and severe edema.

The surgeon encounters a bleeding vessel, visible as a spurter, and controls it using monopolar or bipolar diathermy. A window is created in the posterior Calot's triangle, and further dissection is performed to clearly define this window.

The surgeon identifies and dissects the cystic artery, converting the mid-clavicular port from five millimeters to ten millimeters for better access. The cystic artery is then clipped and cut, completing this step of the procedure.

The surgeon performs further dissection to achieve the critical view of safety, milking the cystic duct to move any stones upwards. The surgical field shows the cystic duct clearly, with no stones visible.

The surgeon places staggered clips on the wide cystic duct, ensuring secure closure before transecting it. Following this, the gallbladder is dissected off the liver using diathermy, completing the separation of the gallbladder from the liver bed.

The surgeon retrieves a single fallen stone using a scoop. After completing the dissection of the gallbladder from the liver, the surgical area is thoroughly washed with normal saline to remove any spilled bile, clots, and blood. The clips on the cystic duct and artery are checked for secure placement.

The surgeon introduces a specimen retrieval bag into the surgical field and places the gallbladder inside it for extraction. A drain is then placed in the subhepatic space, marking the completion of the surgery.
